paragraphs.text: In the 21st century, more and more products we buy exist in digital form: books, music, films, software and video games. However, unlike other formats, video games are being removed from consumers’ access with complete impunity from the companies that sold them. This practice violates basic consumer rights and highlights a legal vacuum that urgently needs to be resolved from any legislation aimed at genuine digital equity. One of the most alarming examples is the recent closure by Ubisoft of the Crew game, which left thousands of users without access to a product they had legitimately bought. While many games require online connection for social or multi-player functions, the worrying thing is that even individual modes or offline experiences are designed in such a way that they cannot be run without active servers. Thus, the product no longer exists for the consumer, as if a book company erases your copy because it no longer compensates for maintaining it. Such situations are at the heart of the European Citizens’ Initiative Stop Killing Games, which in less than a year has exceeded one million signatures to require that the video games sold do not become illegible or unusable when companies decide to stop supporting. The proposal does not require companies to keep servers always, but to enable offline modes where technically possible or to release software so that the community can maintain it through private servers or modifications. We don’t talk about hacking or free access: we talk about retaining access to what one has already paid for. From a legal point of view, this is comparable to buying a film and, years later, the distributor enters your hard disk and erases the file without compensation. This is absurd. However, this practice often occurs in the video game industry, which is covered by opaque service conditions, avoiding any liability. In this context, it might be interesting to consider, as part of the development of the Digital Equity Act, the inclusion of measures that discourage this type of forced obsolescence. It would be desirable for digital consumers to have durable access to the products they have purchased, and for businesses to be aware of the implications of fully removing previously sold content. Incorporating such guarantees could help balance the relationship between users and digital suppliers, avoiding situations where access to a product depends exclusively on unilateral commercial decisions. We therefore propose that this law should provide for: — The consumer’s right to execute any software purchased, even if the official medium has ceased. — An obligation for companies to facilitate offline use or release the necessary elements for the community to maintain basic functionality. — The prohibition of unilateral removal of access to a purchased digital product, except in cases of proven legal infringement. It is not just about protecting digital cultural heritage, but about defending consumer rights in an increasingly digitalised ecosystem. What happens to video games today, tomorrow can happen with other forms of software, educational platforms or even work tools.
